ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to bamboo clam (Solen/Sinonovacula spp.); closest parent 'clam (whole or parts)' used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers clams and cockles as a category; no bamboo-clam-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144007 is the nearest available concept for clam as food; no bamboo-clam-specific SNOMED code identified.

preparation_method: served with garlic and glass noodles

serving.natural_language_portion: One bamboo clam piece as typically served in a Singaporean or Malaysian seafood restaurant weighs approximately 80 g shell-on, yielding roughly 30–35 g of edible meat.

nutrient_source.title: Clam, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15156)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No bamboo-clam-specific entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA SR Legacy raw clam values (NDB 15156) are used as the closest defensible analogue for raw bivalve meat composition. Values represent edible portion per 100 g. Cooking method (steaming, grilling) may alter sodium and moisture; values reflect raw state as a conservative baseline.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for bamboo clams exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Shellfish and lean seafood are consistently reported with very low GI values (GI ≈ 4–5) due to negligible carbohydrate content. GI of 4 is assigned by analogy with other bivalve shellfish (oysters, mussels) as documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (4 × 0.78) / 100 ≈ 0.03, rounded to 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Naturally low sodium in raw state; restaurant preparation may add soy sauce or salt
hypertension.guidance: Raw bamboo clam meat contains approximately 56 mg sodium per 100 g, making it a low-sodium protein source; however, patients with hypertension should request minimal soy sauce or salt-based sauces when ordering at restaurants, as restaurant-style preparation can substantially increase sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bamboo clams contain approximately 2.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g with a very low estimated glycemic index of 4, making them an excellent high-protein, low-glycemic food choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; portion control of accompanying sauces and noodles is advised to limit overall glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High protein, low saturated fat, rich in omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and vitamin B12
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bamboo clams provide lean protein (12.8 g/100 g) with low saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) and are a source of cardioprotective omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and vitamin B12, supporting cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should favour steamed or grilled preparations over those with heavy sauces high in sodium or saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bamboo clams (Solen strictus or Sinonovacula constricta) are elongated bivalve molluscs named for their resemblance to bamboo stalks. They are a prized seafood delicacy across Southeast Asia, particularly in Singapore, Malaysia, and China, where they are commonly served at zi char restaurants and seafood houses. The flesh is tender, slightly sweet, and briny, with a firm yet yielding texture. Bamboo clams are most commonly prepared by steaming with garlic, glass noodles, and soy sauce, or grilled with butter and herbs. In Singapore and Malaysia, they are frequently featured as a premium dish at seafood restaurants, often priced per piece due to their size and quality. Nutritionally, bamboo clams are an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 12.8 g per 100 g edible portion), very low in fat (1.0 g/100 g) and carbohydrates (2.6 g/100 g), and exceptionally rich in vitamin B12 (49.4 mcg/100 g), iron (13.98 mg/100 g), and selenium (24.3 mcg/100 g). Their very low glycemic index (estimated GI ≈ 4) makes them suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The low saturated fat content and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids support cardiovascular health. Sodium content is naturally low in the raw state but may increase significantly with restaurant-style sauces. Bamboo clams are a shellfish allergen and are not suitable for individuals with mollusc allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bamboo clams?
Are bamboo clams good for diabetics?
What is the protein content of bamboo clams?
Are bamboo clams high in sodium?
Are bamboo clams the same as razor clams?
Where can I eat bamboo clams in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No bamboo-clam-specific entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw clam data (NDB 15156), which is the closest defensible approved-source analogue for raw bivalve meat. GI is estimated by analogy with other bivalve shellfish from Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy raw clam (NDB 15156) as the closest approved-source analogue; no bamboo-clam-specific entry was found in regional primary databases. Values represent raw edible meat per 100 g. 2. Restaurant preparation (steaming with soy sauce, garlic, glass noodles) will alter sodium and carbohydrate content relative to raw values. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy clam entry. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy clam entry. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw clam. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw clam. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw clam; clams contain no plant-based fibre. 5. GI of 4 is an analogous estimate based on bivalve shellfish data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for bamboo clams exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Serving size of 80 g (shell-on, one piece) is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion; edible yield is approximately 30–35 g. 7. Halal status reflects the general permissibility of shellfish under Malay-Muslim dietary practice in Singapore and Malaysia, though individual certification may vary by restaurant.
